author: daniellehrner
content: I do not see how that changes to today. With encrypted mempools the wallet still sees the plaintext tx, because it is the one encrypting it on the first place. Why can't it send this plaintext information to a builder same as they do today by sending it to a private mempool? The wallets still can sell this information and whoever has a contract with the wallet will have access to the plaintext tx.

author: data.always
content: you can, but then you don't have an encrypted transaction and there's no reason to put it in LUCID blockspace 🤷‍♂️. if for some reason you did want to leak the info but still put it in that part of blockspace, then the builder also needs to know the plaintext of every transaction in front of it in LUCID blockspace --> and you still have this degraded execution quality from the noise from the entire block.

author: data.always
content: well there still is a reason, you get some force inclusion guarantees.

author: daniellehrner
content:
Today theres is no permissionless way to include tx to Ethereum without being frontrun or sandwiched. Relying on centralised actors means being open to capture, e.g.Flashbots was one of the first to implement the OFAC sanctions of Tornado Cash. 

Nobody can stop wallets to sell user information, like tx, to a 3rd party. But what encrypted mempools allow is to not rely on middle-men and still have MEV protection. I, as user, have the option to do it permissionlessly, but I am not forced to.

The current centralised MEV protection system is starving the public mem pool, which weakens FOCIL. Without encrypted mem pools FOCIL is only useful for txs without MEV or the censored tx is a victim to have their MEV extracted once picked up by FOCIL.

The Fusaka incident showed how fragile the centralised MEV protection is. In your article about it, you showed that txs were simply sent to the public mem pool without any protection when the circuit breakers were triggered. We got lucky that day that no searcher was prepared for that and not much MEV was extracted, the next time this could be different. Encrypted mem pools could at least be fall backs when the centralised players have issues.

author: data.always
content:
I'm not arguing that encrypted mempools are bad, just that this design is bad. If one of your goals is to end the starvation of the public mempool, this design will not meaningfully drive order flow away from private mempools. 

We have years of data from L2s, other L1s, and out-of-protocol encrypted mempools. We should learn from those experiences and design a better system than LUCID.

author: daniellehrner
content: Why not iterating on LUCID instead? It can't be all bad

author: data.always
content: Maybe that's what will end up happening, but why implement a three week old design that clearly is flawed and constrain the solution space?

author: daniellehrner
content: The authors started to work on it in June last year AFAIK
